pr.title: Handle a bad epilogue case discovered in lbvector
pr.author: PsiLupan

summary: Updated the ELF postprocess tool to recognize and patch an additional bad epilogue scheduling case found while working on lbvector. The existing postprocess logic already repaired separated mtlr/blr and old-stack addi/blr epilogues; this PR adds detection of an lwz followed immediately by an mr before blr and swaps the lwz/mr instructions back into the expected order.
what_changed: In impl_postprocess_elf, added tracking variables lwz_pos and mr_pos alongside existing mtlr_pos and addi_pos epilogue tracking.
Added a new mr_epilogues list storing detected bad epilogue cases as position pairs.
Extended instruction scanning to detect lwz r0, N(r1) patterns using mask 0xFFFFFF00 == 0x80010000 with a positive immediate byte.
Added detection for opcode 0x7FE3FB78 immediately after the recorded lwz position, labeled in the diff comment as an mr instruction.
When a blr is encountered, the postprocess now records lwz/mr epilogue candidates if both lwz_pos and mr_pos were seen.
Added a patch loop that reads the lwz and mr instructions and writes them back swapped, printing a diagnostic message: "Patching old lwz/mr epilogue".
Removed the previous final assert requiring mtlr_pos to be zero after scanning, likely because the epilogue state handling was broadened and reset at blr boundaries.
smart_moves: Kept the fix localized to tools/postprocess.py instead of changing generated C or hand-editing assembly, preserving the project workflow where known compiler/codegen quirks are normalized during postprocessing.
Reused the existing epilogue-detection style: scan instructions, record byte offsets, then apply targeted binary rewrites after detection.
Restricted the new mr detection to the instruction immediately after lwz via it == lwz_pos + 4, reducing the chance of patching unrelated instruction sequences.
Used opcode masking for lwz r0, N(r1), allowing the stack offset to vary while still matching the intended instruction family.
Reset lwz_pos and mr_pos at blr along with mtlr_pos/addi_pos, keeping epilogue candidate state scoped to a single return sequence.
decomp_lessons: Postprocess tooling may need to handle multiple distinct compiler scheduling artifacts, not just the first discovered mtlr/blr pattern.
When a bad epilogue pattern appears in one source area such as lbvector, it can be useful to encode the fix as a general binary pattern in tooling so later decompilation work can reuse it.
Instruction-order fixes can often be implemented as offset-based swaps after a conservative scan, avoiding broader changes to matching code.
For epilogue matching, tracking positions until blr gives a simple way to identify return-sequence patterns without building a full disassembler.
Evidence is limited to the title and diff; there were no PR body notes, issue comments, review comments, or reviews in the provided context.
naming_conventions: New position-tracking locals follow the existing *_pos convention: lwz_pos, mr_pos, mtlr_pos, addi_pos.
The new list name mr_epilogues mirrors the existing epilogues collection while distinguishing the lwz/mr repair case.
Patch diagnostics follow the existing imperative style, e.g. "Patching old lwz/mr epilogue".
assembly_or_matching_tactics: Recognized mtlr via exact opcode 0x7C0803A6 and blr via exact opcode 0x4E800020, preserving existing epilogue reunification behavior.
Recognized addi r1, r1, 0x18 via exact opcode 0x38210018 for the existing old-stack epilogue path.
Recognized lwz r0, N(r1) using instr & 0xFFFFFF00 == 0x80010000 and instr & 0xFF > 0, allowing nonzero stack offsets.
Recognized the target mr instruction by exact opcode 0x7FE3FB78 and required it to appear immediately after the lwz candidate.
Patched the bad lwz/mr case by reading both instructions from their recorded offsets and writing them back in swapped order.
The tactic is a narrow post-link binary rewrite, useful for matching compiler output when instruction scheduling differs from desired decompilation output.
follow_up_queries: Find later PRs touching tools/postprocess.py to see whether the lwz/mr epilogue patch was generalized or corrected.
Search for lbvector matching PRs around this date to identify the exact function that exposed the bad epilogue case.
Check whether opcode 0x7FE3FB78 is commented correctly in the final repository and whether future reviewers refined the instruction description.
Look for other postprocess epilogue patches involving mtlr, blr, lwz, mr, or stack restore patterns.
